segments.text: But to Jonah this seemed very wrong, and he became angry.

Isn't this what I said, Lord, when I was still at home? That is what I tried to forestall by fleeing to Tarshish. I knew that you are a gracious and compassionate God, slow to anger and abounding in love, a God who relents from sending calamity.
segments.speaker: Jonah

segments.text: Now, Lord, take away my life, for it is better for me to die than to live.
segments.speaker: Jonah

segments.text: Is it right for you to be angry?
segments.speaker: The Lord

segments.text: Jonah had gone out and sat down at a place east of the city. There he made himself a shelter, sat in its shade and waited to see what would happen to the city.
Then the Lord God provided a leafy plant and made it grow up over Jonah to give shade for his head to ease his discomfort, and Jonah was very happy about the plant.
But at dawn the next day God provided a worm, which chewed the plant so that it withered.
When the sun rose, God provided a scorching east wind, and the sun blazed on Jonah's head so that he grew faint.
It would be better for me to die than to live.
segments.speaker: Jonah

segments.text: Is it right for you to be angry about the plant?
segments.speaker: God

segments.text: It is. And I'm so angry I wish I were dead.
segments.speaker: Jonah

segments.text: You have been concerned about this plant, though you did not tend it or make it grow. It sprang up overnight and died overnight.
segments.speaker: The Lord

segments.text: And should I not have concern for the great city of Nineveh, in which there are more than a hundred and twenty thousand people who cannot tell their right hand from their left—and also many animals?
segments.speaker: The Lord
